text: Amenorrheic female distance athletes have approximately 4.5-fold more bone injuries than eumenorrheic athletes, with a large effect size.
Male distance athletes with low testosterone (lowest quartile of reference range) have approximately 4.5-fold more bone injuries than those with normal testosterone, with a moderate effect size.
Low energy availability defined as less than 30 kcal per kg of fat-free mass per day is associated with impaired reproductive function and lower bone mineral density in distance athletes.
Amenorrheic female athletes and low-testosterone male athletes show significantly lower triiodothyronine (T3) levels, indicating suppressed metabolic function from low energy availability.

Reproductive function markers (menstrual status in females, testosterone concentrations in males) are more objective and accurate indicators of adequate energy availability than dietary and training log-based energy availability calculations.
Athletes categorized as higher risk by Female Athlete Triad or RED-S screening tools had up to 10-fold more training days lost to bone injuries in the preceding year compared to lower-risk groups.

Low testosterone in male distance athletes, defined as the lowest quartile of reference range (mean 15.1 nmol/L), is associated with significantly lower bone mineral density and higher fracture rates.
